coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction and durability of carbon dot-anchored nickel-iron oxyhydroxide electrodes for alkaline water electrolysis at high current densities

Kang, Misook
coredata.dc:description: This study presents a nickel-iron layered double hydroxide (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH) catalyst anchored with carbon dots (CD), optimized for high current density alkaline water electrolysis. The catalyst employs a Ni:Fe ratio of 0.6:0.4, which balances catalytic activity and stability. Fe enhances the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) by promoting oxygen vacancy formation, while Ni supports structural integrity and facilitates electron transfer. Carbon dots improve conductivity, protect the electrode from corrosion, and stabilize active sites. The interplay among Ni, Fe, and C creates a robust electron transfer network, enabling efficient O₂ evolution while suppressing inactive intermediates. As a result, the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH/CP electrode achieves a low overpotential of 1.47 V at 10 mA/cm2 and maintains stable overpotentials of 1.64 V and 1.98 V at 100 and 500 mA/cm2, respectively, over 10 days of continuous operation. These findings highlight the potential of this catalyst design to address key challenges in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) performance and durability, paving the way for scalable and efficient energy conversion technologies.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of various Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes, (b) Comparison of overpotentials required to reach 100 mA/cm2 for different Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes, (c) Tafel slopes of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes, (d) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves measured in the non-Faradaic region at 100 mV/s scan rate, (e) Electrochemical surface area (ECSA) estimation from Cdl values, (f) Turnover frequency (TOF) calculated at 250 mV overpotential, (g) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots, and (h) Chronopotentiometry measurements over 10 h.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) comparison between 1.0 M KOH and 1.0 M TMAOH electrolytes for CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, (b) Raman spectra of CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP in 1 M KOH and 1 M TMAOH, and (c) Schematic comparison of the adsorbates evolution mechanism (AEM) and lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM).

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction and durability of carbon dot-anchored nickel-iron oxyhydroxide electrodes for alkaline water electrolysis at high current densities

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Natural Sciences, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk, 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Natural Sciences, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk, 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a nickel-iron layered double hydroxide (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH) catalyst anchored with carbon dots (CD), optimized for high current density alkaline water electrolysis. The catalyst employs a Ni:Fe ratio of 0.6:0.4, which balances catalytic activity and stability. Fe enhances the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) by promoting oxygen vacancy formation, while Ni supports structural integrity and facilitates electron transfer. Carbon dots improve conductivity, protect the electrode from corrosion, and stabilize active sites. The interplay among Ni, Fe, and C creates a robust electron transfer network, enabling efficient O₂ evolution while suppressing inactive intermediates. As a result, the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH/CP electrode achieves a low overpotential of 1.47 V at 10 mA/cm2 and maintains stable overpotentials of 1.64 V and 1.98 V at 100 and 500 mA/cm2, respectively, over 10 days of continuous operation. These findings highlight the potential of this catalyst design to address key challenges in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) performance and durability, paving the way for scalable and efficient energy conversion technologies.

ce:keyword.ce:text: High current density alkaline water electrolysis

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline water electrolysis is one of the most promising technologies for large-scale hydrogen production, a clean energy source, and is expected to play a critical role in the future energy market due to its economic viability and stability [1]. However, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), which causes the greatest energy loss in the electrolysis process, is the main factor limiting the overall efficiency of the system, making the development of high-performance catalysts essential for its optimization [2]. Precious metal-based catalysts such as RuO₂ and IrO₂ exhibit high activity, but their high cost, low durability, and performance degradation in long-term alkaline environments present significant barriers to large-scale commercialization [3,4]. Therefore, the development of alternative catalysts that are both cost-effective and capable of stable operation in alkaline conditions over extended periods is urgently needed. Transition metal oxides such as MnO₂, NiO, and Fe₂O₃, have emerged as alternatives due to their low cost and relatively high performance [5–7]. However, these catalysts also face limitations in practical applications due to low activity, rapid deactivation, and issues with structural stability. To address these challenges, research into new catalyst structures has become necessary.

Recently, transition metal-based layered structures, particularly NiFeOOH-LDH (Layered Double Hydroxide) combining nickel and iron, have emerged as a promising alternative due to their low cost, high stability, and excellent OER activity [8,9]. The synergistic effect between transition metals and the layered structure provides a large surface area and numerous active sites, resulting in outstanding electrochemical performance [10]. However, at high current densities, structural collapse and limitations in electrical conductivity lead to performance degradation [11]. The intrinsic catalytic properties of NiFe LDH are heavily influenced by the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM), which facilitates oxygen evolution by involving lattice oxygen atoms directly in the reaction pathway. Fe plays a critical role in enabling oxygen vacancies that activate the LOM, while Ni contributes to structural stabilization and electron transfer, resulting in enhanced OER performance [12,13]. However, the practical realization of these mechanisms under extreme operational conditions requires further optimization of the Ni:Fe ratio and the structural integrity of the LDH framework.

To overcome these challenges, new design strategies that enhance the conductivity and maximize the structural stability of the catalyst are required. Among these, carbon-based materials have garnered significant attention due to their ability to enhance electrical conductivity, stabilize active sites, and prevent structural degradation. Specifically, carbon dots (CDs) have been identified as a promising material for anchoring onto NiFe LDH surfaces, forming a robust conductive network that supports efficient charge transfer and suppresses the formation of inactive intermediates [14,15]. In this work, carbon dots derived from sucrose are utilized to anchor and protect the LDH surface, enabling long-term stability even at high current densities. Compared to traditional carbon layers, carbon dots offer enhanced surface coverage and superior interaction with the active metal sites, ensuring both durability and improved catalytic performance under operational stresses [16].

This study focuses on developing NiFeOOH-LDH-based catalysts capable of maintaining stable performance even under extreme current density conditions in alkaline water electrolysis. In particular, an innovative approach was taken by introducing a sucrose-derived carbon protective layer to shield the active sites on the catalyst surface while maximizing conductivity. This protective layer is designed to suppress the formation of inactive intermediates and ensure long-term stable OER performance under extreme conditions such as high current densities. Furthermore, the optimal molar ratio of Ni and Fe was explored to maximize catalytic activity, with performance evaluated at high current densities of 100 and 500 mA/cm2. In addition to improving stability, the synergy between Ni, Fe, and the carbon layer has been demonstrated to establish an efficient electron transfer network, facilitating rapid reaction kinetics and durable operation. This unique design strategy provides insights into the fundamental relationship between catalyst composition, structural features, and electrochemical performance. Ultimately, this study aims to provide a new direction for the development of high-efficiency, highly durable catalysts for next-generation alkaline water electrolysis systems by innovatively enhancing the structural stability and conductivity of NiFeOOH-LDH-based catalysts. This work offers a practical and commercially viable solution for large-scale hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP and CD@Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method, as illustrated in Scheme 1 , where the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalyst particles were directly grown on the surface of carbon paper (CP, MGL-370, AvCarb USA) as a supporting electrode. Initially, the CP electrode was thermally treated at 475 °C for 30 min in a furnace to remove organic impurities from the surface before the catalyst particle growth. For the synthesis of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH, specific amounts of nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO₃)₂·6H₂O, 99.9 %, Junsei Co., Japan) and ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO₃)₃·9H₂O, 99.9 %, Junsei Co., Japan) were weighed according to the desired Ni:Fe molar ratios of 1.0:0, 0.9:0.1, 0.8:0.2, 0.7:0.3, 0.6:0.4, and 0.5:0.5, and added to 50 mL of deionized water. To promote the formation of the layered structure and enhance oxidation to Ni(III) (NiOOH), 7.5 mmol of NH₄F (99.99 %, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was added. Additionally 16.0 mmol of urea (CO(NH₂)₂, Junsei Co., Japan) was used as both a pH adjuster and oxidizing agent to ensure uniform particle size and high-purity LDH (Layered Double Hydroxide) formation. The solution was stirred vigorously for 3 h, after which the pre-treated CP electrodes (cut to dimensions of 10 mm × 10 mm × 1.5 mm) were immersed in the solution for 30 min. The well-stirred solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed, and heated at 120 °C for 12 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the autoclave was opened, and the catalyst-coated CP electrodes were removed with tweezers, washed twice with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven set at 60 °C for 24 h. As a result, six types of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes were obtained with final Ni:Fe molar ratios of 1:0, 0.9:0.1, 0.8:0.2, 0.7:0.3, 0.6:0.4, and 0.5:0.5, ensuring that the total dosage of Ni(NO₃)₂ and Fe(NO₃)₃ was kept constant at 5 mmol. The amount of catalyst grown on the CP electrodes, measured by the weight difference before and after growth, was confirmed to be approximately 11 mg, indicating consistent catalyst particle loading.

To prepare carbon dot-anchored (expressed as CD@) Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes, a 0.05 M sucrose solution was added to a 20 mL vial, and the above synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes were immersed in the solution. The vial was then placed in a vacuum oven set at 30 °C for 30 min, after which the temperature was raised to 70 °C to evaporate the solvent. The temperature was further increased to 200 °C and maintained for 2 h to complete the fabrication of CD@Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalyst particles grown on CP electrodes and electrochemical activity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalyst particles grown on the CP electrodes was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Miniflex, Rigaku, Japan) using Ni-filtered CuKα radiation with a nickel filter (30.0 kV and 15.0 mA) over a 2θ range of 5–90°. The morphology and surface structure of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalyst particles grown on the CP electrodes were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-7600, Hitachi Ltd., Japan) and a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan). Elemental analysis and mapping of the catalyst components were performed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, EX-250, Horiba). The elemental composition of the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalyst was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements using AlKα (1486.6 eV) radiation on an AXIS-NOVA system (Kratos Inc., USA). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the catalyst particles scraped off the CP electrodes was determined by N₂ adsorption at liquid nitrogen temperatures, using a continuous flow of N₂ and He as carrier gases. The samples were degassed under vacuum at 150 °C for 1 h before being introduced into the liquid nitrogen adsorption system (Bel Japan).

The electrochemical activity of the electrodes was measured at room temperature using an electrochemical cell test system (IVIUMnSTAT, Ivium Technologies, Netherlands). The electrochemical oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activity was tested in an alkaline electrolyte (1.0 M KOH, pH = 14) using a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell, and the overpotential was obtained using the equation: VRHE = VSCE + 0.241 + 0.059 × pH. The CP electrodes with grown catalyst particles served as the working electrodes, a saturated calomel electrode (Hg/Hg₂Cl₂, SCE) was used as the reference electrode, and a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode. To stabilize the electrodes, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were recorded at 1.0–1.7 V (vs. RHE) for 100 cycles. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted in 1.0 M KOH solution with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed over a frequency range of 0.01–100,000 Hz with an AC amplitude of 5 mV. Chronopotentiometry was used to evaluate the stability and durability of the electrodes at current densities of 10, 100, and 500 mA/cm2, monitoring the overpotential to ensure its stability over time. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the electrocatalysts was determined by CV in the non-faradaic region (0.96–1.06 V vs. RHE) at various scan rates. All electrochemical measurements were corrected for iR drop compensation using the automatic current interrupt method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of Ni:Fe ratio based on physical, electrochemical properties, and OER performance of Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the crystallographic structures of the synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained for samples with varying Ni-Fe ratios (Fig. 1 a, Fig. S1). The diffraction peaks observed for all samples correspond to the standard Ni-LDH phase (JCPDS No. 40–0215) [17], indicating the successful formation of the layered double hydroxide (LDH) structure. Prominent diffraction peaks at approximately 11.5°, 23.2°, and 34.5° correspond to the (003), (006), and (012) planes, respectively, confirming the typical layered structure of LDH [18]. The XRD patterns of all samples show similar peak positions, suggesting that substitution of Fe into the Ni-LDH lattice does not significantly alter the overall crystal structure. However, slight shifts in peak positions and intensity changes were observed as the Fe content increased, indicating minor lattice distortions and changes in crystallinity. Specifically, when Fe is incorporated into the Ni LDH structure, the (003) and (006) diffraction peaks shift slightly toward higher angles. This subtle shift indicates a slight decrease in the interlayer spacing. These changes arise due to the difference in ionic radii between Ni2⁺ and Fe³⁺, which causes subtle changes in the interlayer distances of the LDH structure. This can be attributed to the increased disorder in the stacking sequence of the hydroxide layers as Fe ions replace Ni ions in the lattice. Despite these variations, the overall diffraction patterns confirm the retention of the LDH structure in all samples, demonstrating the robustness of the structure even with changes in metal composition.

The functional groups and bonding in the synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH samples were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1b, Fig. S2). Absorption bands observed in the 400–700 cm⁻1 region correspond to metal-oxygen (M − O) stretching vibrations, characteristic of the LDH structure. The absorption peak at 551 cm⁻1 is assigned to Ni-OH stretching vibrations, while the peak at 628 cm⁻1 corresponds to Fe-OH stretching modes [19]. These peaks confirm the presence of both Ni and Fe within the layered double hydroxide (LDH) structure. The Fe-OH peak becomes more pronounced as the Fe content increases, indicating a higher concentration of Fe in the lattice. Broad absorption bands observed at 1640 cm⁻1 and between 3000 and 3500 cm⁻1 correspond to the bending vibrations of interlayer water molecules, hydroxyl stretching vibrations, and hydroxyl groups within the LDH material, which are commonly observed in LDH structures [20]. The peak at 1380 cm⁻1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of nitrate ions. Given that LDH structures possess ion-exchange properties, interlayer anions such as nitrate or carbonate can be incorporated into the material, likely arising from the nitrate salts used as precursors during synthesis.

To investigate the nanostructure of the synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH samples, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were analyzed (Fig. 1c, Fig. S3). A typical two-dimensional nanoplates structure was observed, consistent with the layered structure of Ni-Fe oxyhydroxide (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH). While the overall morphology appears spherical, closer inspection reveals thinly stacked nanoplates, with the thickness of the nanoplates decreasing and the irregularity of the layer structure increasing as the Fe content increases. This is likely due to weaker interlayer interactions and a more dispersed structure as the Fe content increases. These changes in the arrangement of the nanoplates are expected to influence the physical properties and electrochemical performance of the catalyst. Specifically, the increase in Fe content may lead to a larger surface area and an increase in active sites, potentially enhancing the electrochemical reactivity of the material. When Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH is grown on nickel foam, the nanoplates exhibit a more interconnected growth pattern, indicating that the growth form and thickness of the nanoplates depend on the substrate electrode (Fig. S4). The SEM images demonstrate the successful growth of Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH on the porous structure of Ni foam (Fig. S4a). At low magnifications (100 μm and 30 μm), the interconnected and porous network of the Ni foam is shown, with the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH particles uniformly coating the surface. At higher magnifications (2 μm and 400 nm), the characteristic flake-like morphology of LDH is evident, revealing a well-defined and layered structure that ensures a high surface area and abundant active sites. It is worth noting that the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve of Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH grown on Ni foam (Fig. S4b) exhibits a performance comparable to that of the catalyst grown on carbon paper (CP). Both configurations achieve a high current density of approximately 100 mA/cm2 under similar overpotential conditions (1.67 V). This indicates that the excellent catalytic performance of Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH is largely attributed to the intrinsic properties of the catalyst itself, rather than being significantly dependent on the type of substrate. The ability to achieve comparable OER performance on different support materials, such as Ni foam and carbon paper, highlights the versatility of the fabrication process and the robustness of Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH as a high-performance catalyst for alkaline water electrolysis. This finding demonstrates that the catalyst can be effectively applied to a wide range of substrate materials, broadening its potential for practical applications.

The elemental composition of the synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH samples was analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 1d, Fig. S5). The EDS spectra clearly show the presence of Ni, Fe, and O peaks, confirming the elemental composition of the samples. In the spectrum of NiOOH-LDH, only strong Ni and O peaks are observed, corresponding to the pure NiOOH-LDH structure. In contrast, the samples from Ni₀.₉Fe₀.₁OOH-LDH to Ni₀.₅Fe₀.₅OOH-LDH show progressively stronger Fe peaks. As the Fe content increases, the relative intensity of the Ni peak decreases while that of the Fe peak increases. This confirms that Fe is replacing Ni in the synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalyst, as shown by the chemical composition in the table. Interestingly, as the Fe content increases, the oxygen (O) peak also becomes stronger, indicating a higher degree of oxide and hydroxide layer formation within the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH structure, particularly in the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH [21].

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were used to examine the surface area and porosity of the synthesized Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH (Fig. 1e, Fig. S6). The average pore diameter was calculated as Dp = 4VT/S, where VT is the total pore volume and S is the BET surface area [22]. Except for the NiOOH-LDH, which exhibited a type II isotherm characteristic of non-porous or microporous materials, most samples displayed typical type IV isotherms, indicative of mesoporous structures [23]. At low relative pressures (0.0 < P/P₀ < 0.2), monolayer adsorption of gas molecules was predominant, while at higher relative pressures (P/P₀ > 0.8), capillary condensation occurred. The hysteresis loop observed in the desorption curve indicates delayed gas desorption in mesopores, with this effect being more pronounced in the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH and Ni₀.₅Fe₀.₅OOH-LDH. Overall, an increase in BET surface area (adsorption capacity) was observed as the Fe content increased, with Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH showing the highest surface area. This suggests that Fe substitution influences the pore structure and the overall surface area, which is an important physical property related to the catalytic performance, as higher surface area can enhance reactant adsorption during OER.

The oxidation states of Ni, Fe, and O in Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) before the reaction (Fig. S7). The Ni 2p spectrum of NiOOH-LDH without Fe shows peaks at 855.6 eV and 873.1 eV, corresponding to the Ni 2p₃/₂ and Ni 2p₁/₂ states, respectively, indicating the oxidation state of Ni2⁺ [24]. Additionally, satellite peaks at 861.8 eV and 879.92 eV indicate the presence of Ni(OH)₂ [25]. The O 1s spectrum shows three main peaks at 529.6 eV, 531.1 eV, and 532.6 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (M − O), metal hydroxide (M − OH), and surface-adsorbed oxygen species, respectively [26]. The Fe 2p spectrum indicates a mixed valence state of Fe2⁺ and Fe³⁺. As Fe is incorporated, additional peaks at 856.4 eV and 873.9 eV appear in the Ni 2p spectrum, corresponding to Ni³⁺. This suggests that Fe incorporation facilitates the partial oxidation of Ni2⁺ to Ni³⁺, resulting in Ni2⁺/Ni³⁺ and Fe2⁺/Fe³⁺ states [27]. Additionally, the Ni 2p₃/₂ and Ni 2p₁/₂ peaks shift to lower binding energies, while the Fe 2p₃/₂ and Fe 2p₁/₂ peaks shift to higher binding energies. These shifts in binding energy reflect a redistribution of electron density between Ni and Fe atoms, signifying strong electronic interactions. According to previous studies, when Fe2⁺ is incorporated into the Ni sites in Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH, stable Fe2⁺-O-Fe³⁺ pairs form, enhancing the intrinsic OER activity of the material [28]. Furthermore, the O 1s peak associated with Ni-O-Fe and Fe-O-Fe shifts to lower binding energies than in Ni-O-Ni, indicating that the oxygen binding environment has been altered due to Fe substitution. A lower binding energy suggests that the oxygen species are more electron-rich, making them more susceptible to participation in OER reactions, particularly through the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM), which was more pronounced in Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH [29].

To determine the optimal Ni:Fe ratio, the electrochemical properties of the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH catalysts grown on CP electrodes were evaluated, and the results are presented in Fig. 2 . The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) performance of five different Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes was assessed using linear polarization curves in 1.0 M KOH solution. The potentials required to reach a current density of 100 mA/cm2 for the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes with x = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 were 1.721, 1.705, 1.698, 1.681, 1.695 V, respectively (Fig. 2a). The corresponding overpotentials (η) were 491, 475, 468, 451, and 465 mV for x = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5, respectively, with the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode showing the best performance (Fig. 2b). The slope of the Tafel plot in the linear region is related to the reaction mechanism or the rate-determining step (RDS) of the OER, with a smaller Tafel slope indicating a faster reaction rate and a more efficient catalyst [30]. The Tafel slopes for the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH/CP electrodes with x = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 were 93, 89, 86, 78, and 83 mV/dec, respectively, with the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP showing the lowest Tafel slope of 78 mV/dec (Fig. 2c). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed at various scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, 100 mV s⁻1) in the non-faradaic region (Fig. S8). The nearly rectangular shape of the CV curves in this region indicates capacitive charging due to the electrical double layer (Cdl), suggesting that no redox reactions occur in this region [31]. As the scan rate increased, the current density increased for all electrodes, indicating that higher scan rates lead to larger current responses. At a scan rate of 100 mV s⁻1, the current density increased with increasing Fe content, reaching a maximum for x = 0.4 (Fig. 2d). The increase in current density with Fe substitution indicates that Fe plays an effective role in the OER reaction, particularly for the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode, which represents the optimal composition. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA), calculated from the Cdl derived from CV, was 18.76, 31.70, 34.54, 35.51, 47.56, and 40.33 mFcm⁻2 for the NiOOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₉Fe₀.₁OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₈Fe₀.₂OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₇Fe₀.₃OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, and Ni₀.₅Fe₀.₅OOH-LDH/CP electrodes, respectively, with the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode having the largest ECSA (Fig. 2e). Turn over frequency (TOF) is an intrinsic property of the catalyst and an important indicator of catalytic performance, defined as the number of molecules reacting at active sites per unit time and calculated as the average number of molecules converted per site over a given time period [32]. The TOF values for Ni₀.₉Fe₀.₁OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₈Fe₀.₂OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₇Fe₀.₃OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, and Ni₀.₅Fe₀.₅OOH-LDH/CP were 0.073, 0.184, 0.384, 0.469, and 0.385 s⁻1, respectively, with Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP showing the highest TOF of 0.469 s⁻1 (Fig. 2f). The increase in TOF reflects superior catalytic performance for OER, and this enhanced performance is attributed to the interaction between Ni and Fe and the presence of more exposed catalytic active sites. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plot is shown in Fig. 2g, where the x-axis represents the real part of the impedance (Z′) and the y-axis represents the imaginary part (−Z″). The semicircle in the Nyquist plot represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), and the smaller the diameter of the semicircle, the lower the resistance. The differences in the size of the semicircle correspond to varying Ni:Fe ratios, with increasing Fe content leading to lower resistance, indicating superior electrochemical performance for the electrodes. Finally, the stability of the electrodes was evaluated at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm2 over 10 h (Fig. 2h). The potential increased by 0.013 V for the NiOOH-LDH/CP electrode, but Ni₀.₉Fe₀.₁OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₈Fe₀.₂OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₇Fe₀.₃OOH-LDH/CP, Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, and Ni₀.₅Fe₀.₅OOH-LDH/CP electrodes showed minimal degradation, indicating that the LDH structure exhibits excellent durability during prolonged OER tests.

To assess the durability of the best-performing Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH catalyst, 3000 linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) cycles were performed in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte (Fig. 3 a), followed by a 10-day chronopotentiometry test at a current density of 10 mA cm⁻2 to monitor the potential changes over time (Fig. 3b). The Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP showed stable potentials during low current operation without performance degradation over repeated cycles. However, at higher current densities, a notable shift in potential was observed. Typically, lab-scale academic OER studies focus on measuring the potential required to reach a current density of 10 mA cm⁻2, but for industrial applications, catalysts must maintain low potentials at much higher current densities, at least 100 mA cm⁻2. Therefore, although the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP demonstrates good durability at this stage, further improvement is necessary for high-current operation. The chronopotentiometry experiment at 10 mA cm⁻2 showed an initial potential of 1.478 V. However, from the 5th day onward, the potential began to increase, reaching 1.506 V after 10 days, indicating a performance degradation over time. This deterioration may have been caused by changes in the catalyst structure or the active surface, leading to the decision to coat the surface with carbon to stabilize the catalyst.

Thus, while the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode exhibits excellent initial OER performance, the results of the 3000-cycle LSV test and the 10-day chronopotentiometry experiment reveal that the catalyst undergoes some degradation when exposed to a highly alkaline environment for an extended period, resulting in reduced OER performance. To address this issue, the surface of the optimized Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH was anchored with carbon dots (CD) to enhance the stability. Thermal treatment was applied at 200 °C to promote strong interactions between the LDH surface and the carbon. The thermal stability of the Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH-LDH at 200 °C was confirmed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. S9). The TGA data reveal two distinct stages. In the first stage, mass loss occurs up to 200 °C due to the evaporation of water molecules adsorbed on the catalyst and in the interlayers, without structural collapse. In the second stage, the interlayer anions are removed, and the layered structure transitions into mixed metal oxide phases. These results indicate that the thermal stability of the LDH structure is maintained below 225 °C [33].

To further investigate the carbon dot-anchored CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode, we compare Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP and CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP. To evaluate the OER catalytic performance, LSV, Tafel slope, ECSA, and durability tests were conducted and are presented in Fig. 4 . In the LSV test results (Fig. 4a), both Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP and CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP samples exhibited significantly lower potentials for OER activation compared to pure NiOOH-LDH/CP. Particularly, the carbon dot-anchored CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP showed the highest current density at the lowest potential, indicating superior performance in OER activation. As shown in Fig. 4b, the overpotentials (η) at a current density of 100 mA cm⁻2 were 451 mV (1.681 V) for Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP and 408 mV (1.638 V) for CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, demonstrating that carbon dot anchoring significantly improved the OER performance of the catalyst. The Tafel slope analysis (Fig. 4c) revealed that CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP had the lowest Tafel slope of 72 mV/dec, indicating the fastest electrochemical reaction rate among the samples. This suggests that carbon dot anchoring enhances the reaction kinetics and improves the electron transfer characteristics of the catalyst. Although Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP also showed an excellent performance with a Tafel slope of 78 mV/dec, the carbon dot-anchored sample exhibited even better performance. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl), calculated from the CV in the non-faradaic region (Fig. S10), was used to estimate the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the electrodes (Fig. 4e). CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP showed the highest ECSA of 63.50 mF/cm2, indicating the presence of a greater number of active catalytic sites. Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP also exhibited a high ECSA of 47.56 mF/cm2, but the surface area was further increased by carbon dot anchoring. The durability of the electrodes was evaluated at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 over a period of 10 h (Fig. 4f). CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP maintained stable performance without significant degradation over time, indicating that carbon dot anchoring significantly enhances the long-term durability of the catalyst. While Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP also demonstrated excellent durability, the carbon dot-anchored sample exhibited superior stability. Turnover frequency (TOF) results (Fig. 4g) at 250 mV and 440 mV demonstrated the catalytic activity of the samples. At 250 mV, CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP showed a TOF of 0.526 s⁻1, while at 440 mV, it exhibited a TOF of 4.68 s⁻1, both higher than those of Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP. This indicates that carbon dot anchoring accelerates the reaction rate at the active sites, leading to improved catalytic performance.

Meanwhile, Table S1 [34–43] compares the electrochemical performance of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP sample with various previously reported OER catalysts, summarizing the overpotentials (η at 10 mA/cm2), substrates, and electrolyte conditions for each catalyst. The performances listed in the table were all recorded under the same 1.0 M KOH electrolyte conditions, making it a suitable reference for quantitatively comparing catalyst performance. The CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP sample in this study exhibited a lower overpotential compared to previously reported catalysts such as NiFe LDH-Ni(III)III (248 mV) [34] and NiFe LDH-0.2Mn (270 mV) [36], confirming that carbon dot anchoring significantly enhances the catalytic performance. Additionally, compared to other catalysts using nickel foam substrates, the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP also demonstrated superior performance. For example, it outperformed FeCoNi-LDH (269 mV) [43] and t-NiCoFe-LDH (277 mV) [42]. These results highlight the positive impact of carbon dot anchoring on the OER performance of NiFe-based catalysts.

To investigate the structural, chemical, and electrochemical properties of carbon dot-anchored CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH, we conducted X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analyses, as shown in Fig. 5 . In the XRD patterns of the LDH active materials grown on the CP substrate, the characteristic peaks of the LDH were not clearly visible (Fig. 5a). This is due to the strong crystalline carbon peak of the CP substrate (2θ = 26°). However, in the magnified XRD pattern, a prominent peak corresponding to the (003) plane was observed in all samples, indicating that the layered double hydroxide (LDH) structure was successfully formed on the CP. Despite carbon dot anchoring, this peak remained intact, confirming that the carbon dot anchoring does not disrupt the LDH crystal structure. In the FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 5b, both CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH and Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH display peaks below 500 cm⁻1 corresponding to the typical metal-oxygen (M − O) bonds. The Ni-OH peak at 551 cm⁻1 and the Fe-OH peak at 628 cm⁻1 confirm the structural integrity of the catalyst. Additionally, in the carbon dot-anchored sample, peaks corresponding to C-H and C=C bonds were observed, indicating the successful incorporation of carbon. Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 5c) was performed to further confirm the carbon dot anchoring. The D-band and G-band peaks were observed, and the ID/IG ratio of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP sample was calculated to be 0.43, indicating that while the structure is not fully crystalline, the carbon forms a partially graphene-like structure. These peaks were not observed in the non-carbon dot-anchored Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH sample. The EIS results (Fig. 5d) were used to evaluate the electrochemical resistance of the catalysts. The CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP sample exhibited the smallest semicircular Nyquist plot, indicating the lowest charge transfer resistance. In contrast, NiOOH-LDH/CP showed relatively higher resistance. Lower resistance corresponds to higher electrical conductivity, which is a crucial factor in OER performance. High conductivity facilitates electron transfer between the catalyst surface and the current collector, reducing energy losses. The anchored carbon helps to fill the area near the Fermi level with electrons from carbon, creating a metal-like state that enhances electronic conductivity [44]. SEM images (Fig. 5e, Fig. S11) reveal the surface morphology of each electrode. The CP substrate has a smooth surface, while NiOOH-LDH/CP exhibits a relatively thick NiOOH-LDH flake covering the CP. In both Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP and CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, thinner flake structures are observed, and the carbon dot-anchored sample appears to have a denser surface structure. This is further confirmed by TEM images (Fig. 5f, Fig. S12), which show that rather than encapsulating the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH flakes, the carbon dot anchoring resulted in small carbon quantum dots attached to the surface of the flakes. This demonstrates that the nanoflakes transformed into an aggregated structure, and irregular particle structures formed due to carbon dot anchoring. These particle structures may contribute to increasing the surface area and enhancing the electrochemical reactivity.

The microstructure and elemental distribution of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Fig. 6 a shows the bright-field TEM image of CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, revealing that the sample exhibits a flower-like nanostructure. The nanoflakes are radially arranged, and the presence of carbon dots embedded on the surface of the nanoflakes is clearly visible. The nanoflakes have an approximate diameter of 1 μm, and the carbon dots embedded on the nanoflake surfaces measure less than 10 nm in size. These nanoflakes are uniformly grown, and the carbon dots embedded in the nanoflakes have an interplanar spacing of 0.341 nm, suggesting a graphitic structure. The carbon dots are bonded with Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH, improving electrical conductivity, which plays a crucial role in maximizing the catalytic performance. In Fig. 6b, various lattice spacings were observed. The growth of the (006), (101), (015), and (018) planes of LDH is evident, and the lattice spacings were measured as 0.384 nm, 0.268 nm, 0.231 nm, and 0.198 nm, respectively. Specifically, the lattice spacing of the (006) plane was measured to be 3.842 Å, which is slightly reduced compared to the Ni LDH structure [45]. This is consistent with the results observed from XRD. The SAED pattern analysis shows diffraction rings that correspond to these crystal planes, confirming the polycrystalline nature of the LDH (Fig. 6d) [46]. High-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) elemental mapping (Fig. 6c) confirmed the uniform distribution of Ni, Fe, and O across the sample, with carbon dots evenly distributed on the surface. The uniform distribution of carbon dots across the nanoflake surface facilitates efficient electron transfer and enhances the structural stability of the catalyst, likely maintaining its performance during long-term electrochemical reactions. This is directly related to the catalyst's durability, acting as a crucial factor in preventing performance degradation during extended oxygen evolution reaction (OER) testing.

The long-term durability of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the high stability of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode was confirmed through 3000 cycling tests. Initially, there was a slight performance improvement during the first 500 cycles, followed by stable operation over the next 3000 cycles. By the 3000th cycle, the electrode maintained excellent catalytic performance with minimal change compared to the initial performance, indicating the outstanding durability of the sample. The Faradaic efficiency measured over 5 h (Fig. 7b) was 97.2 %, demonstrating that almost all the applied current contributed to oxygen evolution, confirming the high electrochemical efficiency of the sample. The SEM images taken before and after 3000 cycles (Fig. 7c) show no noticeable structural changes, confirming that the electrode surface remained well-preserved and stable under operating conditions. This is an important characteristic that ensures the catalyst's long lifespan. The evaluating OER performance at a high current density of 500 mA/cm2 is crucial, as it provides insights into the catalyst's durability, efficiency, and long-term stability. Particularly, when the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH is protected by carbon dot, it becomes important to assess how effectively this structure prevents electrochemical corrosion and maintains stable operation under high current density without performance degradation. As shown in Fig. 7d, chronopotentiometry tests were conducted for 10 days at various current densities of 10, 100, and 500 mA/cm2. The CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode maintained the same potential as the initial value over 10 days at all three current densities. It is very encouraging that the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode operates stably even at a very high current density of 500 mA/cm2 and the potential of 1.98 V remains unchanged for 10 days. In contrast, the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode without carbon dot anchoring exhibits a significant increase in potential over time as the current density increases. This provides clear evidence that carbon dots effectively protect the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode, even under high current density conditions in alkaline solution, helping to maintain its excellent durability. Additionally, a low potential of 1.64 V was observed at 100 mA/cm2, meeting the requirements for industrial applications. Fig. 7e presents SEM images for CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP after the 10-day durability test. Even after testing at current densities of 10, 100, and 500 mA/cm2, the sample maintained a stable nanostructure without any structural damage. This indicates that the catalyst can be used for extended periods under high current conditions. To demonstrate the anchored carbon dot remains intact, Fig. S13 shows TEM images of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP catalyst after a 10-day long-term OER stability test conducted at 100 mA/cm2. The images confirm that both the structural integrity of the LDH and the carbon dot (CD) anchoring remained intact, demonstrating excellent durability under harsh alkaline conditions. At lower magnification, the overall morphology of the LDH flakes is preserved, indicating that the layered structure of the catalyst experienced minimal degradation after prolonged OER operation. At higher magnification, the carbon dots are clearly visible as small, uniformly distributed dark spots on the surface of the LDH flakes. This observation confirms that the carbon dot anchoring was not detached or damaged during the test, highlighting its chemical and physical stability. These results emphasize the robustness of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode, where the carbon dots play a critical role in maintaining the structural and chemical stability of the catalyst, even after prolonged exposure to OER conditions. This stability underscores the potential of the catalyst for long-term application in alkaline water electrolysis. Video S1 shows a real-time video of the OER reaction over 10 days at 100 mA/cm2, where the overpotential remained constant throughout the test.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtadv.2025.100560

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical property of CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP before and after OER, and understanding the OER mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively elucidate the role of carbon-dot anchoring in stabilizing the oxidation states of Ni and Fe, XPS analysis was performed for Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP without carbon-dot anchoring before and after a 10-day OER stability test under a high current density of 100 mA/cm2 (Fig. S14). The results revealed pronounced changes in the oxidation states of Ni and Fe under these conditions. In the Ni 2p spectra, the ratio of Ni³⁺/Ni2⁺ increased significantly post-reaction, with the area for Ni³⁺ escalating from 37,455 a.u. to 60,952 a.u., while the Ni2⁺ area decreased from 48,231 a.u. to 41,723 a.u. This indicates substantial oxidation of Ni during the OER process, highlighting its susceptibility to over-oxidation in the absence of carbon dots. Similarly, the Fe 2p spectra exhibited a marked increase in the Fe³⁺ peak area from 30,245 a.u. to 48,732 a.u., while the Fe2⁺ area showed only a slight change from 25,105 a.u. to 27,162 a.u. These findings demonstrate that Fe is similarly vulnerable to oxidation, albeit to a lesser extent than Ni, under prolonged OER conditions. In addition, the O 1s spectra for Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP without carbon-dot anchoring showed a dramatic increase in the lattice oxygen (M − O) peak area from 12,626 a.u. to 35,372 a.u. This significant change suggests enhanced lattice oxygen participation in the OER process. However, this also points to potential structural instability, as excessive lattice oxygen involvement is often associated with the degradation of layered double hydroxides (LDH) during the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM).

In contrast, the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode exhibited much smaller changes in the oxidation states of Ni and Fe after the OER process, as confirmed by high-resolution XPS (Fig. 8 ). The Ni 2p spectra showed a shift to higher binding energies, indicative of the oxidation of Ni2⁺ to Ni³⁺ (Ni(OH)₂ → NiOOH). Similarly, the Fe 2p spectra revealed a shift corresponding to the oxidation of Fe2⁺ to Fe³⁺ (Fe(OH)₂ → FeOOH). These shifts confirm that both Ni and Fe actively participate in the OER process. The formation of oxyhydroxide phases, such as Fe-OOH-Ni, likely plays a critical role in the reaction mechanism [47]. These oxyhydroxides significantly lower the energy barrier for chemisorbed intermediates, thereby enhancing OER performance [48]. Notably, the changes in the oxidation states were far less pronounced in the carbon-dot-anchored sample, underscoring the stabilizing role of the carbon dots. Carbon dots mitigate over-oxidation by enhancing electron conductivity and ensuring the redistribution of electron density, which stabilizes the mixed oxidation states (Fe2⁺/Fe³⁺ and Ni2⁺/Ni³⁺). This effect is further evident in the O 1s spectra, where the increase in the lattice oxygen peak area was controlled, indicating reduced lattice oxygen participation in the LOM. This moderation directly prevents structural collapse of the LDH, a common consequence of excessive lattice oxygen involvement in the LOM. The influence of carbon dots is particularly evident when comparing the behavior of the O 1s lattice oxygen peak. In Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP without carbon dots, the pronounced changes in the lattice oxygen peak suggest a strong LOM pathway, which could lead to structural instability. Conversely, in the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP, the moderated lattice oxygen peak indicates that carbon dots effectively mitigate structural degradation while maintaining high catalytic activity. This stabilization allows the LOM to proceed without compromising the structural integrity of the catalyst, ensuring long-term durability. Furthermore, the incorporation of Fe into Ni-LDH promotes the formation of mixed oxidation states, sustaining redox reactions over extended periods. The redistribution of electron density, further stabilized by the carbon-dot layer, enhances the chemical and structural robustness of the catalyst. These findings collectively demonstrate that carbon-dot anchoring plays a pivotal role in achieving superior OER performance and long-term durability under industrially relevant conditions. The comparison between Fig. S14 and Fig. 8 highlights the importance of carbon-dot anchoring in maintaining the chemical and structural stability of the Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH catalyst, effectively mitigating the adverse effects of the LOM while enhancing the overall OER performance. These results underscore the potential of carbon-dot-anchored catalysts for scalable and durable applications in alkaline water electrolysis.

This provides evidence that the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) mechanism in the NiFeLDH/CP electrode is likely governed by the lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) rather than the adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) [49]. While many studies have indicated that the OER process on metal oxides proceeds via the AEM pathway [50], characterized by the continuous adsorption and transformation of oxygenated intermediates (M∗→ M–OH; M − O → M–OOH), the findings in this study reveal a significant deviation. In the AEM, hydroxide ions (OH⁻) are activated on the catalyst surface, leading to the release of oxygen (O₂) through intermediate stages involving M − OH deprotonation and M–O–OH desorption. In contrast, the LOM introduces the direct involvement of lattice oxygen in the reaction, with the mechanism proceeding as follows [51]:
1.
Hydroxide adsorption: Hydroxide ions (OH⁻) or water (H₂O) from the electrolyte adsorb onto the catalyst surface.
2.
Lattice oxygen activation: Oxidation of surface metal ions to higher valence states weakens the bonds with lattice oxygen, rendering it mobile and reactive.
3.
Lattice oxygen oxidation: The activated lattice oxygen undergoes oxidation, with oxygen atoms combining to form molecular oxygen (O₂). This process involves the breaking of bonds between oxygen and metal, facilitating oxygen release.
4.
Lattice oxygen replenishment: Hydroxide ions from the electrolyte replenish the lost lattice oxygen, stabilizing the catalyst structure for continued OER activity.

To validate the LOM mechanism, the OER performance of the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP electrode was investigated in 1.0 M KOH and 1.0 M TMAOH electrolytes. Tetramethylammonium (TMA⁺) ions were employed as a chemical probe to suppress the LOM pathway by strongly interacting with critical intermediates, such as peroxo (O₂2⁻) and superoxo (O₂⁻), which are vital for lattice oxygen participation in OER. Fig. 9 a illustrates the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves, showing a significant reduction in OER performance in 1.0 M TMAOH compared to 1.0 M KOH. This reduction suggests that TMA⁺ ions effectively inhibited lattice oxygen oxidation by binding to essential intermediates, thereby disrupting the LOM pathway [52]. Supporting this observation, Raman spectra (Fig. 9b) of CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP after exposure to TMAOH show distinct peaks at 752 cm⁻1 and 952 cm⁻1, indicative of TMA⁺ interaction with lattice oxygen [53]. The interaction altered the Eg bending vibration (δ(M − O)) and Ag stretching vibration (ν(M − O)) peaks, further corroborating the disruption of the LOM pathway. A comparative analysis presented in Fig. 9c highlights the differences between AEM and LOM mechanisms. The AEM relies on metal ions as the primary active sites, while the LOM features direct lattice oxygen participation. During OER via LOM, lattice oxygen is oxidized to release O₂, with oxygen vacancies formed and subsequently replenished. Under TMAOH conditions, the strong interaction between TMA⁺ and lattice oxygen significantly impedes the LOM process, leading to reduced OER efficiency.

These results emphasize that, unlike the conventional metal-centered AEM, the LOM pathway predominantly involves lattice oxygen as the reactive species, thereby providing a distinct reaction mechanism for NiFeLDH/CP electrodes [54–56]. The TMA⁺ probe experiment unequivocally demonstrates the pivotal role of lattice oxygen in driving the OER process, advancing the understanding of fundamental catalytic mechanisms in LDH-based systems.

This study demonstrates that the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP catalyst exhibits exceptional stability and performance in OER under high current densities, which is crucial for practical and industrial applications. The catalyst maintained its structural integrity and catalytic activity over 10 days of continuous operation at current densities up to 500 mA/cm2, with minimal overpotential degradation. This high stability at elevated current densities ensures that the CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP catalyst can meet the rigorous demands of industrial OER processes, such as in large-scale water electrolysis for hydrogen production. Additionally, the presence of carbon dots in the catalyst enhanced its conductivity and durability, contributing to long-term stability even under harsh electrochemical conditions. While the lattice oxygen mechanism played a role in facilitating the reaction, the catalyst's ability to maintain high performance under industrially relevant conditions is its most attractive feature.

These findings highlight the potential of CD@Ni₀.₆Fe₀.₄OOH-LDH/CP as a high-performance, durable catalyst for OER in energy conversion technologies. Future research should focus on further optimizing the catalyst for even higher current densities and exploring its scalability for commercial applications. This work paves the way for developing robust catalysts that ensure both efficiency and stability in next-generation energy systems.

Hyunsub Shin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jaehun Lee: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation.
